title: Cell specialization
summary: One fertilised egg becomes trillions of specialised cells, yet every one of them carries the same genome. This lesson works through how that happens: differentiation as differential gene expression (the same genes, but only a subset switched on), how a specialised cell's structure is matched to its function in named examples (red blood cell, muscle fibre, sperm, palisade mesophyll, root hair cell), the potency of stem cells (totipotent, pluripotent, multipotent) and their sources (embryonic, adult/tissue and induced pluripotent), the therapeutic use of stem cells and the ethical debate around them, and the organisational hierarchy from cells to tissues to organs to systems. Every worked example is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 marks — point by point, with error-carried-forward — so you can see how each creditable idea protects your score.
learningObjectives: Explain cell differentiation as the result of differential gene expression, given that all somatic cells share the same genome.
Relate the structure of named specialised cells (e.g. red blood cell, muscle fibre, sperm, palisade mesophyll, root hair cell) to their functions.
Distinguish totipotent, pluripotent and multipotent stem cells and identify their sources (embryonic, adult/tissue and induced pluripotent).
Outline the therapeutic use of stem cells and evaluate the ethical considerations they raise.
Describe the hierarchy of organisation from cells to tissues to organs to organ systems.
Present an explanation so that each distinct, valid marking point is credited and error-carried-forward protects the answer.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: One Genome, Many Cells
simpleExplanation.summary: All the cells in your body descend from a single fertilised egg and carry an identical set of genes, yet a neuron, a muscle fibre and a white blood cell could hardly look more different. The difference is not in which genes a cell has, but in which genes it switches on.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Picture a huge library in which every reading room holds an identical copy of the same collection of books. To turn one room into a 'kitchen' you leave only the cookbooks open; to turn another into a 'workshop' you leave only the tool manuals open. No books are ever removed — each room specialises purely by choosing which volumes to read. In the same way, a specialised cell keeps the whole genome but expresses only the subset of genes its job requires. That selective reading is cell differentiation through differential gene expression.
steps: Every somatic cell in an organism contains the same complete genome — the same genes.
During development a cell receives chemical signals from its position and its neighbours.
Those signals switch certain genes on (expressed) and keep others off (silenced) — differential gene expression.
The expressed genes are transcribed and translated into a specific set of proteins.
Those proteins build the cell's specialised structures and carry out its specialised functions — the cell has differentiated.

flashcards.back: The process by which an unspecialised cell develops a specialised structure and function. It occurs through the expression of some of the cell's genes but not others — no genes are lost.

Different genes are switched ON or OFF in different cells, so each cell makes a different set of proteins. This — not a difference in the genes present — is what produces different cell types from one genome.

flashcards.front: Do specialised cells lose unused genes?
flashcards.back: No. This is a classic misconception. All somatic cells keep the ENTIRE genome; genes that a cell does not need are silenced (not expressed), not deleted.
An unspecialised (or partially specialised) cell with two defining properties: SELF-RENEWAL (it can divide to make more stem cells) and POTENCY (it can differentiate into specialised cell types).
Can differentiate into EVERY cell type, including the extra-embryonic tissues such as the placenta. Only the zygote and the cells of the very early embryo are totipotent.
Can differentiate into any cell type of the body proper (all three germ layers) but NOT the extra-embryonic tissues. Embryonic stem cells from the inner cell mass of a blastocyst are pluripotent.
Can differentiate into a LIMITED range of related cell types within one tissue. Adult/tissue stem cells such as haematopoietic stem cells in bone marrow (which form the blood cells) are multipotent.

Adult somatic cells (e.g. skin cells) reprogrammed back into a pluripotent state by switching on specific genes. They avoid the ethical objections to embryonic stem cells and can be patient-specific, reducing immune rejection.
Biconcave shape (large surface-area-to-volume ratio for gas exchange); no nucleus and no mitochondria (more room for haemoglobin, and no O₂ used up); packed with haemoglobin to bind and carry oxygen.
A long, thin extension (the root hair) greatly increases the surface area for absorbing water and mineral ions from the soil; a thin wall keeps the diffusion/osmosis distance short.
Specialised CELLS of one type group into TISSUES; different tissues combine into ORGANS; organs working together form ORGAN SYSTEMS; systems together make up the ORGANISM.

flashcards.front: Ethical issue with embryonic stem cells
flashcards.back: Obtaining them destroys a blastocyst, which some regard as a potential human life. The debate weighs the potential to treat disease against the moral status of the embryo; iPSCs are one way to sidestep it.
Differentiation is differential gene EXPRESSION, not the loss or mutation of genes. Writing that specialised cells 'lose' the genes they don't use loses the mark.
Only totipotent cells can form extra-embryonic tissue (placenta). Embryonic stem cells are PLURIPOTENT, not totipotent — a distinction examiners test directly.

quickCheck.prompt: How does a liver cell come to differ from a neuron, given both carry the same genome?
options: The liver cell has lost the genes for being a neuron
Each cell expresses a different subset of the same genes
The neuron has extra genes the liver cell never received
Their genomes mutated in different directions during development
quickCheck.answer: Each cell expresses a different subset of the same genes

quickCheck.prompt: A stem cell that can form any cell of the adult body but NOT the placenta is best described as:
options: Totipotent
Pluripotent
Multipotent
Unipotent

quickCheck.prompt: Which source provides MULTIPOTENT stem cells?
options: The inner cell mass of a blastocyst
A zygote after its first division
Adult bone marrow (haematopoietic stem cells)
Reprogrammed adult skin cells

quickCheck.prompt: The mature mammalian red blood cell has no nucleus. The main advantage of this is that it:
options: Divides more quickly to replace lost cells
Has more room to carry haemoglobin and therefore oxygen
Can change its shape to become any other cell type
Uses the extra space to store mitochondria for energy
quickCheck.answer: Has more room to carry haemoglobin and therefore oxygen

quickCheck.prompt: Which point is central to the ETHICAL objection to embryonic stem cells?
options: Obtaining them destroys a blastocyst that some consider a potential human life
Embryonic stem cells are multipotent and so of limited use
They can only be taken from the patient's own body
They cannot differentiate into specialised cell types
quickCheck.answer: Obtaining them destroys a blastocyst that some consider a potential human life

faq.q: If every cell has the same genes, what actually decides which ones are expressed?
faq.a: A cell's fate is set by the signals it receives — its position in the developing embryo, chemical signals (morphogens) from neighbouring cells, and the regulatory proteins already present. These switch particular genes on and others off. Because expression is selective, the cell builds only the proteins its role needs, and it is those proteins — not any change to the DNA itself — that make one cell a neuron and another a muscle fibre.

faq.q: What is the difference between pluripotent and multipotent stem cells?
faq.a: It is about how wide their range is. Pluripotent cells (e.g. embryonic stem cells) can become any cell type of the body proper — all three germ layers — but not extra-embryonic tissue such as the placenta. Multipotent cells (e.g. haematopoietic stem cells in bone marrow) are more restricted: they form only a limited family of related cell types within one tissue. Totipotent cells are wider still than pluripotent, adding the extra-embryonic tissues.

faq.q: Are there alternatives to using embryonic stem cells?
faq.a: Yes — the leading one is induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). Adult somatic cells such as skin cells are reprogrammed back to a pluripotent state by switching on a small set of genes. Because no embryo is destroyed, iPSCs avoid the main ethical objection, and because they can be made from the patient's own cells they lower the risk of immune rejection. Adult (tissue) stem cells, though only multipotent, are another established source.

faq.q: Why do multicellular organisms bother with specialised cells at all?
faq.a: Specialisation allows a division of labour. A single cell that had to do everything would do each job poorly, but a cell that expresses only the genes for one task can be built to do it extremely well — a red blood cell packed with haemoglobin, a muscle fibre packed with contractile filaments. Specialised cells of the same type then group into tissues, tissues into organs, and organs into systems, letting the organism grow large and complex while each part stays efficient.

sections.content: Every multicellular organism begins as a single cell — the zygote. Through repeated division that one cell gives rise to the trillions of cells of an adult body, and those cells are far from identical: neurons, muscle fibres, red blood cells and palisade mesophyll cells are each built for a particular job. The striking part is that they all carry the SAME genome. This lesson explains how a single set of genes produces so many kinds of cell (differentiation by differential gene expression), how each specialised cell's structure fits its function, where stem cells come from and what they can become, and how cells are organised into tissues, organs and systems. As in every course lesson, the worked examples are set out the way our marking engine actually awards the marks, because in an explain-type question it is the distinct, named points that keep your score.
Differentiation: one genome, different genes expressed

All of the diploid somatic cells in an organism contain the same complete set of genes — the **genome**. So if the instructions are identical, how do cells become so different? The answer is **differential gene expression**. At any moment only a subset of a cell's genes is active ('expressed'); the rest are switched off ('silenced'). **Cell differentiation** is the process in which a cell becomes specialised by expressing a particular subset of its genes. The genes that are switched on are transcribed and translated into a specific set of **proteins**, and those proteins determine the cell's structure, its metabolism and therefore its function. Crucially, silenced genes are still physically present — nothing is deleted. A liver cell and a neuron differ because they read different parts of the same instruction book, not because they own different books.

items: All somatic cells in an organism share one identical genome — the same genes.
Differentiation is a cell becoming specialised through **differential gene expression**.
Only a subset of genes is expressed; the rest are silenced but NOT lost.
The expressed genes are translated into specific proteins, which give the cell its structure and function.

sections.content: Structure matched to function in specialised cells

Because a specialised cell expresses only the genes its job requires, its structure ends up finely matched to its function. This structure–function relationship is a recurring exam theme, and the reliable way to answer it is to link a specific feature to a specific job. A few standard examples: a **red blood cell** is biconcave (large surface-area-to-volume ratio for gas exchange) and loses its nucleus and mitochondria (more room for haemoglobin and no oxygen consumed); a **muscle fibre** is packed with contractile protein filaments and many mitochondria to supply ATP for contraction; a **sperm cell** carries a flagellum for swimming, many mitochondria in the midpiece to power it, and an acrosome of enzymes to penetrate the egg; a **palisade mesophyll cell** is column-shaped and full of chloroplasts near the leaf's upper surface to absorb light for photosynthesis; and a **root hair cell** has a long thin projection that greatly increases surface area for absorbing water and mineral ions.

items: **Red blood cell:** biconcave shape (high surface area for O₂ diffusion); no nucleus/mitochondria (maximum haemoglobin, no O₂ used).
**Muscle fibre:** many contractile filaments and mitochondria — force and ATP for contraction.
**Sperm cell:** flagellum for movement, midpiece mitochondria for ATP, acrosome enzymes to penetrate the egg.
**Palisade mesophyll:** column shape and many chloroplasts near the top of the leaf — maximum light absorption for photosynthesis.
**Root hair cell:** long thin extension — large surface area for absorbing water and mineral ions.

sections.question: Explain how the structure of a red blood cell is adapted to its function of transporting oxygen. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer** — each line links a feature to the job it does.

A red blood cell is packed with the protein haemoglobin, which binds oxygen, so each cell can carry a large amount of oxygen. It has a biconcave shape, which gives a large surface-area-to-volume ratio and so speeds the diffusion of oxygen into and out of the cell. It has no nucleus (and no mitochondria), which leaves more internal space for haemoglobin and means the cell does not use up the oxygen it carries. Its small, flexible shape also lets it squeeze through narrow capillaries to deliver oxygen to respiring tissues.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct feature-linked-to-function point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (A):** contains haemoglobin, which binds/carries oxygen.
- **Point 2 (A):** biconcave shape → large surface-area-to-volume ratio → faster oxygen diffusion.
- **Point 3 (A):** no nucleus (and/or no mitochondria) → more room for haemoglobin / oxygen not consumed.
- **Point 4 (A):** small and flexible → passes through narrow capillaries to reach tissues.

The marks are for the LINK, not the feature alone. 'It is biconcave' on its own scores nothing; 'it is biconcave, giving a large surface area for oxygen to diffuse across' scores the point. Because more than four creditable links are available, the engine caps the award at 4 — you can miss one and still reach full marks.

sections.content: For any 'explain the adaptations' question, write in the form 'Feature X allows function Y because…'. A bare list of features earns little; the marks are for connecting each structural feature to the job it performs. State the feature, then the consequence, then the function.

**Stem cells** are unspecialised cells with two defining properties: **self-renewal** (they divide to make more stem cells) and **potency** (they can differentiate into specialised cells). Potency describes HOW MANY types of cell a stem cell can become, and it narrows as development proceeds. **Totipotent** cells can form every cell type including extra-embryonic tissue such as the placenta — only the zygote and the very early embryo are totipotent. **Pluripotent** cells can form any cell of the body proper but not the placenta; embryonic stem cells from the inner cell mass of a blastocyst are pluripotent. **Multipotent** cells form only a limited family of related cell types within one tissue; adult (tissue) stem cells such as the haematopoietic stem cells of bone marrow, which make the blood cells, are multipotent. Stem cells come from three main **sources**: embryonic stem cells (from early embryos), adult/tissue stem cells (from tissues such as bone marrow), and **induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs)**, made by reprogramming a patient's own adult cells back to a pluripotent state.

items: **Totipotent:** any cell type INCLUDING extra-embryonic tissue (placenta). Source: zygote / very early embryo.
**Pluripotent:** any cell of the body proper but NOT extra-embryonic tissue. Source: embryonic stem cells (inner cell mass of blastocyst); also iPSCs.
**Multipotent:** a limited range of related cell types. Source: adult/tissue stem cells, e.g. bone-marrow haematopoietic stem cells.
**Sources overall:** embryonic, adult/tissue, and induced pluripotent (reprogrammed adult cells).
Potency narrows as development proceeds — totipotent → pluripotent → multipotent.

sections.content: Therapeutic use of stem cells and the ethics

Because stem cells can both self-renew and differentiate, they can be used to replace cells lost to injury or disease — the basis of regenerative medicine. Two IB examples: in **Stargardt's disease**, an inherited condition in which retinal cells of the macula degenerate and vision is lost, stem cells can be directed to become healthy retinal pigment epithelium cells and injected into the retina to slow or reverse the loss. In **leukaemia**, a cancer of the blood-forming tissue, chemotherapy destroys the cancerous cells in the bone marrow and multipotent haematopoietic stem cells (from a matched donor or the patient's own stored cells) are transplanted to re-establish a healthy population of blood cells. Set against this potential are real **ethical considerations**. Embryonic stem cells are the most versatile but obtaining them destroys a blastocyst, which some regard as a potential human life; there are also risks of tumour formation and of immune rejection of donor cells. iPSCs and adult stem cells reduce these concerns — iPSCs avoid destroying embryos and, being patient-derived, lower rejection risk — which is a large part of why they are so actively researched.

items: **Stargardt's disease:** stem cells directed into retinal pigment epithelium cells, injected to replace degenerated retinal cells and preserve vision.
**Leukaemia:** chemotherapy kills cancerous marrow cells; transplanted haematopoietic (multipotent) stem cells rebuild healthy blood.
**Benefit:** potential to repair or replace tissues that the body cannot regenerate on its own.
**Ethical/practical concerns:** destruction of embryos for embryonic stem cells; risk of tumour formation; immune rejection of donor cells.
**iPSCs/adult stem cells:** avoid destroying embryos and (for iPSCs) lower rejection risk, but iPSCs carry their own reprogramming and tumour-risk questions.

sections.question: Outline the use of stem cells in the treatment of Stargardt's disease. [3]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Stargardt's disease is caused by the degeneration of retinal cells (the retinal pigment epithelium / photoreceptors) in the macula, leading to progressive vision loss. Stem cells are directed to differentiate into healthy retinal pigment epithelium cells in the laboratory. These cells are then injected into the patient's retina, where they replace the damaged cells with the aim of halting or reversing the loss of vision.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 3 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 3):

- **Point 1 (A):** identifies the cause — degeneration of retinal (RPE/photoreceptor) cells causing vision loss.
- **Point 2 (A):** stem cells are made to differentiate into healthy retinal (RPE) cells.
- **Point 3 (A):** these cells are transplanted/injected into the retina to replace the damaged cells / restore vision.

Equivalent wording is accepted (e.g. 'retinal cells' for RPE). A mark is given for stating the SOURCE or the specialised cell produced and a mark for what it REPLACES — be specific rather than saying stem cells 'cure' the disease.

sections.content: Therapeutic-use questions usually reserve a mark for the SOURCE of the stem cells (embryonic, adult/bone marrow, or induced pluripotent) and a mark for the specialised cell they are turned into and replace. Name both. When a question asks you to evaluate, give a benefit AND an ethical or practical drawback.

Specialisation only pays off if specialised cells are organised together. Cells of the same type that carry out a shared task form a **tissue** (for example, muscle tissue or the palisade mesophyll of a leaf). Different tissues combine into an **organ** that performs a defined function (the heart, the leaf, the stomach). Organs that co-operate in a common process make up an **organ system** (the circulatory system, the digestive system). Together the systems form the whole **organism**. Each level up the hierarchy — cell → tissue → organ → system — represents an increasing division of labour, and it is this layered organisation that lets a large multicellular organism keep every part efficient.

items: **Cells:** specialised units, each expressing the genes for one role.
**Tissues:** groups of similar cells doing a shared task (e.g. muscle, palisade mesophyll).
**Organs:** several tissues combined to perform a function (e.g. heart, leaf).
**Organ systems:** organs working together on one process (e.g. circulatory, digestive).
**Organism:** all systems together — the sequence is cell → tissue → organ → system → organism.
**Saying specialised cells 'lose' unused genes** — differentiation is differential gene EXPRESSION; every somatic cell keeps the whole genome, and unused genes are silenced, not deleted.
**Calling embryonic stem cells 'totipotent'** — they are PLURIPOTENT. Only the zygote and very early embryo, which can also form extra-embryonic tissue, are totipotent.
**Listing features without linking them to function** — 'it is biconcave' scores nothing; you must connect the feature to the job ('…giving a large surface area for oxygen diffusion').
**Confusing pluripotent and multipotent** — pluripotent forms any body cell; multipotent forms only a limited related family within one tissue.
**Treating stem cells as a finished 'cure'** — you must describe the mechanism: differentiate into a named specialised cell, then transplant it to replace the damaged one.
**Ignoring the ethics or giving only one side** — 'evaluate' expects a benefit AND an ethical/practical drawback (e.g. destruction of the embryo, tumour risk, rejection).
**Jumbling the hierarchy** — the order is cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism; a tissue is not an organ.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

B2.3 explain-type questions are marked analytically: each distinct, valid biological point is worth one mark, up to the total available. Answer marks (A) credit a correct step in the reasoning, and error-carried-forward (ECF) means one weak line does not sink the rest, provided each idea is written down separately. Equivalent wording is accepted. Study how each mark below is tied to a specific, named idea rather than to loose phrasing.

sections.question: Explain how cells with identical genomes become specialised to carry out different functions. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Every somatic cell in the organism contains the same complete genome, so all cells have the same genes. During differentiation only certain of these genes are expressed in a given cell while the others are switched off. The genes that are expressed are transcribed and translated to produce a specific set of proteins in that cell. These proteins build the cell's specialised structures and carry out its particular functions, so cells that express different genes develop into different, specialised cell types even though their DNA is identical.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each distinct valid point scores 1 (maximum 4):

- **Point 1 (A):** all cells contain the same genes / the same genome.
- **Point 2 (A):** differentiation means only certain genes are expressed (others are switched off) — differential gene expression.
- **Point 3 (A):** the expressed genes produce a specific set of proteins.
- **Point 4 (A):** those proteins give the cell its specialised structure / function.

**ECF and equivalents.** The points are independent, so a student who omits the protein step but correctly states 'same genome' and 'only some genes expressed' still banks those marks — there is no all-or-nothing penalty, and each valid line stands on its own. Equivalent wording is accepted ('genes are silenced' = 'switched off'; 'gene products' = 'proteins').

**What the engine rejects.** Writing that specialised cells 'lose' or 'delete' the genes they do not use scores ZERO for that idea, because differentiation is a change in EXPRESSION, not a change in the genes present. A vague 'the cells just develop differently' with no mention of gene expression earns nothing.

**Bottom line:** to secure all 4 marks, make four distinct statements — same genome → only some genes expressed → specific proteins made → proteins build the specialised structure/function. Repeating the same idea in two forms scores once.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, with ECF and a cap at the marks available. So write in distinct, creditable statements: name the shared genome, name differential gene expression, name the proteins produced, then link them to the specialised structure or function. Each separate idea is a separate mark, and showing every step is what protects your score.

Differentiation by differential gene expression is the foundation for much of what follows in the course: the control of gene expression, the way tissues and organs develop, and the promise and problems of stem-cell medicine. Carry two habits forward — first, that a specialised cell's structure is always readable as a set of adaptations to its function, and second, that 'explain' answers are built from distinct, named points. Both will keep earning marks well beyond this topic.
